title: My Dad Was My Wine Mentor. Now, I Collect for My Kids
summary:
Wine collecting isn’t really about building a cellar. It’s about taking something that naturally evolves over time and tying it to the people you love.

The post My Dad Was My Wine Mentor. Now, I Collect for My Kids appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There’s a moment when a wine collection stops being about what’s in the bottle and starts becoming about who you’ll share it with. For me, that realization didn’t happen all at once. It crept in over time, somewhere between collecting bottles for myself and realizing I was quietly, yet purposefully, collecting for my kids.

When my son and daughter were born (2010 and 2012 respectively), I started setting aside wines from those vintages. At the time, it felt like a thoughtful gesture, maybe even a little romantic. But now, with them almost 14 and 16, those bottles feel like something more. It’s not just a case of wine on a shelf for each of them any longer; they’re placeholders for moments that haven’t happened yet. 

If I’m being honest, my dad is the reason I think this way about collecting. He’s the one who introduced me to wine (and maybe a little bit younger than he should have, but that’s another story.) There wasn’t some formal sit-down where he taught me how to swirl and sniff. It was simpler than that…It was about food on the table and an aged bottle of Riesling he was enjoying with it. Or a wicker-based Chianti that he and my grandpa were enjoying over some freshly made pasta and gravy. Sure, there were probably a couple of early (supervised!) sips along the way. But it wasn’t about “drinking” and partying it up. It was about paying attention to what was in the glass and talking about all the sensory experiences that we were noticing. Wondering why one wine tasted different from another. 

Even before I could fully understand it, I could feel that wine meant something more to him. Eventually, it started to mean something more to me, too. That’s the part that I think gets overlooked by many. You don’t fall in love with wine because someone hands you a great bottle that is worth hundreds of dollars… although that certainly doesn’t suck.  You become enthralled with it through moments, meaningful conversations, and emotions. And more often than not, those moments happen with a wine mentor. For me, that someone was my dad.

Tasting Together

Some of my favorite memories with my father are tied to bottles we’ve shared over the years—wines from Burgundy, the Rhône, and Napa Valley. We’ve opened bottles from Gevrey-Chambertin (my epiphany wine that we shared as a family) and Gigondas that turned into long conversations, debates, and the occasional friendly argument about what we were tasting. What started as him teaching me slowly turned into something more balanced as our wine knowledge and passion reached the same level. There are times now where I’ll offer him the first glass of wine at an occasion, and he will ask me what it is. My response is almost always… “You tell me, pops.” And more often than not, he still gets it right!

Just like sports, wine helped us connect in a way that didn’t feel forced. It created space to spend time together, to share something, to learn from each other. And somewhere along the way, it became our thing. Now, as a father myself, I think about that more than I probably expected to. My kids aren’t there yet, although a supervised and educated sip may or may not still be enjoyed. But those birth-year bottles are still resting, evolving slowly in the cellar, just waiting for the right opportunity to enjoy them with my kiddos. I already find myself thinking about the day we’ll open them and taste what has been collected on their respective shelves. Not because I expect some life-changing tasting note (although that would be awesome!), but because I know precisely what that moment can represent.

In the Shop

EuroCave Pure L Dual Zone Wine Cellar

In Stock | $5,595

Shop Now

Twenty-plus years in a properly stored cellar, protected from heat, light, vibration, oxygen, and odors, can turn a bottle into something complex and layered. But even more than that, it turns it into a shared experience that’s been building over time. And the truth is, they might not love it right away. Aged wine isn’t always the most popular wine for new drinkers, as it doesn’t typically have that big concentrated fruit or power. Most often, bottle age will bring about elegance, refinement, and some form of earthy or dried fruit notes. But that’s not really the point. The point is the realization that what’s in the glass has been there, waiting, for as long as they’ve been alive. 

Maybe it turns into a shared interest and maybe, if you’re lucky, it turns into something bigger and more meaningful. Trips to wine regions, walking vineyards together, tasting through new wines. Maybe one day you’re sitting in a tasting room in Napa Valley or the Finger Lakes with your kids, or wandering through a village in Burgundy, picking out bottles not just to drink, but to save for their own future moments.

I’ve seen how that plays out over time with my dad. The conversations are different and the dynamic has shifted. There are moments where I’m still learning from him, and others where I can challenge him and expand his knowledge and appreciation. It’s not about who’s right, it’s always about the experience of figuring it out together.

The Right Bottles with the Right People

Then, there’s the part no one really talks about when they start collecting: you’re just not going to be able to drink everything you’ve collected over the years. At some point, you realize the cellar you have been building over decades wasn’t just for you. These bottles are meant to be opened, shared, and, in some cases, passed on. Some of the best bottles I’ve had weren’t the most expensive or the rarest… they were the ones opened at the right time with the right people.

If I’m lucky, collecting doesn’t stop with me. Maybe one day it’s not just me and my kids at the dinner table with a fabulous bottle of wine, but my dad, too (and my mom, of course). Three generations, popping open one of my kids’ birth-year wines, and having a spirited and passionate conversation not only about the wine, but about the year on the label and all of the wonderful memories that came along with it.

At the end of the day, wine collecting isn’t really about building a cellar. It’s about taking something that naturally evolves over time and tying it to the people you care about most. And if you do it right, those bottles don’t just age well… they can bring your family closer together than you may have ever imagined, one glass at a time.

More Wine Coverage

  • Why stop at tasting or collecting? Take the kids on your next wine trip too.
  • Regan Stephens’ latest story offers a peek into life as a postpartum drinks professional.
  • New research suggests that organic and biodynamic farming can actually improve yields and resilience.
  • Quiet luxury has arrived in Napa Valley, and Mondavi’s latest redesign is proof.
  • Need some summer wine inspo? Our wine reviewers share the bottles they’re reaching for this summer.

title: In Defense of the Vodka Martini
summary:
While my taste in wine has evolved over the years, my martini preference has not, much to the surprise of my Wine Enthusiast colleagues.

The post In Defense of the Vodka Martini appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Wine is nice, but when I want to dance with oblivion, nothing will satisfy my desire like a Grey Goose martini straight up with three blue cheese olives, if you have them. Not dirty; more like the Diet Coke of dirty, with just enough olive brine to keep the vodka and vermouth in the spotlight.

My martini order is a point of controversy among my beverage-industry peers who are willing to die on the hill of purism. The original martini, after all, calls for gin. “Gin is complex.” “Gin is an art.” “Gin is superior in every way.” These are just some of the words of mass destruction I’ve heard in response to my vodka preference. I agree with the sentiment and respect their passion. I often enjoy gin in cocktails—just not in martinis.

The first time I ordered a martini was many years ago at a Greenwich Village dive bar. Back then, I bought $15 bottles of wine from the bin next to the checkout and ordered wine by the glass in basement bars, served warm and poured to the rim. But that night was different, and I needed something more. I was two years into my post-college working life, feeling like a young racehorse stuck behind the gate, running in place. I needed relief, escape, transcendence. So when the server came to our table, I repeated the order I had grown up hearing my mom say at countless restaurants. Anything my parents did seemed sophisticated at the time, and now that I was a grown-up with a grown-up job and grown-up stress, it was time to do grown-up things like drink Grey Goose martinis straight up with three olives.

While my taste in wine has evolved over the years, my martini preference has not, much to the surprise of my Wine Enthusiast colleagues. I recently went on a martini crawl of Manhattan with our spirits reviewer, Kara Newman. Naturally, she’s Team Gin, but not annoying about it, so she was the perfect guide. My mission: Drink the best gin martinis—in Midtown, because I am a suburbanite with kids who doesn’t want to take the train home late at night (sure, another reason to judge my tastes)—to see if they could convert me.

I sampled four distinct gin martinis: olive brine-rinsed at Hawksmoor; cucumber-and pink-pepper-infused with an oyster on the side at The Tusk Bar; double-chilled and unfiltered at Seed Library; olive bitters and rosemary at Drink. The closest I came to joining Team Gin was at Hawksmoor, where they merely rinsed the tin with olive brine before mixing in Sipsmith gin and very dry vermouth, so it was only somewhat dirty. They gave me extra olives on the side, bathing in brine, so I could dirty up the experience to my liking. The olives were meaty and velvety, which the gin cut through like a new dance partner waiting for their chance. This gin martini sang a new harmony to a tune I’ve heard before—floral notes, briny at first, with a citrusy aftertaste.

One of the many incredible bartenders I met that evening said something that stuck with me. She prefers gin martinis because “I like to think about what I’m drinking.” And that’s exactly my point, too. Despite the undeniable beauty and complexity of gin and all the nuanced notes it delivers, I still prefer a vodka martini. Because sometimes, you don’t want to think. Sometimes, you just need the comfort of what is familiar. A drink that warms your body and eases your mind with a song you already know the words to. So for now, it’s vodka on repeat.

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2026 California Issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

title: Give Your Dad the Perfect Father’s Day Gift: Wine and Staying Home
summary:
This Father's Day, bring the winery to dad with an at-home wine tasting.

The post Give Your Dad the Perfect Father’s Day Gift: Wine and Staying Home appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Bringing the tasting room experience home is a fun way to turn your standard Father’s Day celebration into something more memorable. But some at-home wine tastings feel a little bit tedious, almost like homework. There are too many bottles, too many rules, and one person inevitably saying they’re “getting” an aroma and another person internally panicking that the only thing they smell is grapes. 

For the wine-loving dad, you’ll want to plan something a little more playful. 

“The main question I ask myself before I host any gathering is: how do I want my guests to feel?” says Sarah Tracey, sommelier and lifestyle expert at The Lush Life. That shift from what you’re pouring to how people experience it is what separates an enjoyable tasting from a table full of dirty glasses and lingering confusion over the term “minerality.” The ideal at-home tasting should have just enough structure that people can compare, share, and maybe even surprise themselves.

To upgrade this year’s Father’s Day celebration (and side-step the hassle of a reservation or excursion), follow these six steps for the perfect home wine tasting.

Start with a Point of View

“Before hosting an at-home wine tasting, think about the overall goal,” says Eléonore Latour, chief brand officer at Maison Louis Latour. “Whether it’s educational, social, or simply exploratory, [this] will guide your selection and format.”

A smart approach is to think about your bottle selection thematically. This could look like comparing the same grape across a few regions, exploring how inexpensive and premium selections show up differently in the glass, or simply selecting a few offbeat grapes that don’t always show up on wine lists or store shelves.  

Tracey builds tastings around a feeling first and lets that dictate everything from the wine selection to the playlist. “If I want them to feel festive, maybe I’ll stack my lineup with more sparkling wines,” she says. “If I want them to feel excited, I’ll pick lesser-known regions they probably haven’t tried before.”

Retailers tend to approach their wine recommendations the same way. “I usually like to recommend beginning with what is most familiar, but really lean into what is unfamiliar,” says David Gregory, co-owner of Portland wine shop Ora et Labora, underscoring the importance of fostering discovery in a successful at-home wine tasting.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Aria Short Stem Universal Tasting Glass Set of 4

In Stock | $120

Shop Now

Build a Thoughtful Lineup

“The most important thing to think through when hosting a wine tasting is your guests,” says Brianne Cohen, a Los Angeles-based wine educator and event producer. “Their wine vibe and overall vibe.”

From there, keep things simple and focused. Pouring around five wines is the sweet spot, according to Tracey. “It’s enough to give some range, but not so many that it’s overwhelming.”

Cohen recommends building in a little contrast. “One sparkling, two whites, and two reds is a good starting point,” she says. This structure gives the tasting a little rhythm without overcomplicating things. 

Gregory also suggests thinking about a tasting as a series of pathways, rather than a single theme. A lineup might start with a familiar grape, then branch into different directions, comparing regions, winemaking styles, or similar grape varieties. For Pinot Noir lovers, that could mean tasting a bottle from Oregon alongside versions from Burgundy, Germany, or New Zealand, or using it as a springboard for exploring other lighter-bodied reds.

In the Shop

Wine Tasting Spittoon

In Stock | $34.95

Shop Now

Set the Table

You don’t need custom tasting mats, printed guides, or a perfectly styled tablescape to host a successful wine tasting (although those things are also fun). You do, however, need to make it easy for people to participate.

“Have the wine setup be welcoming and easy,” says Cohen. “All wines should already be open, glassware close by. Don’t make your guests have to work for it.”

For a casual tasting, a simple setup with one glass per person, a central place for the wines, a dump bucket or individual spittoons, and water within reach keeps the focus where it should be.

Tracey agrees that a few key elements matter far more than anything decorative. “Wine glasses and a chilling bucket are the main things I have ready to go,” she says, noting that a large bin filled with ice and water can quickly bring wines down to temperature if needed. Water is also essential, both for staying hydrated and for resetting the palate in between wines. 

If you’re opting for a more formal, professional-style setup, there are plenty of printable tasting mat templates and other resources online to help you duplicate the guided tasting format. Whichever setup you choose, be sure to have key information about each wine accessible. While you don’t need full technical specs, things like the region, grape variety or varieties, abv, retail price, and where you got it are always helpful.

In the Shop

Monogrammed Bonded Leather Wine Tasting Journal-Three Initials

In Stock | $49.95

Shop Now

Pour with a Plan

While the goal of an at-home wine tasting is to keep things relaxed, a little structure in how the wines are served can make a noticeable difference in how your guests experience them.

“If I were to offer one recommendation, it would be to progress from lighter to fuller-bodied wines,” says Latour, noting that this helps preserve the ability to taste nuance as the lineup progresses.

In practical terms, this might mean starting with sparkling or crisp whites, moving into richer whites like Chardonnay, Rhône-style blends, or oaked whites, and finishing with reds, with the most structured or intense wines saved for last. 

Pour size matters, too. A modest two-ounce pour is usually enough to give guests time with each wine without overwhelming them or running out too quickly. Make sure to remind guests that they are always welcome to dump wines they either didn’t enjoy or are ready to move on from—no need to finish every last drop before tasting the next in the lineup. 

Serving temperature plays a role as well, but it doesn’t need to be as exacting. Whites should be sufficiently chilled but not ice cold, while reds benefit from being slightly cooler to the touch, rather than room temperature. “It’s not a hill I die on,” says Tracey. “The focus should be on fun and enjoyment here.”

In the Shop

Vacu Vin Wine Host Set (4-Piece) – Graphite

In Stock | $59.99

Shop Now

Offer Interesting Prompts

One of the fastest ways to make a wine tasting feel tedious is to put guests on the spot with questions about what they’re smelling or tasting in technical terms. For most people, this is akin to asking everyone to go around the room and share one interesting fact about themselves. 

Instead, the best wine tastings give people easier, more intuitive ways to interact and share what they’re experiencing. Tracey often leans into prompts that have nothing to do with traditional tasting notes. “If this wine were a song, what would it be?” she will ask. “If you could share this wine with one person in history, who would it be and why?”

Questions like these open up the conversation and make it easier for everyone to participate, regardless of experience level. They also usually lead to more memorable answers and open the door for guests to feel comfortable asking questions and even taking a stab at some technical analysis of the wines, if interested. 

Latour takes a similarly approachable view. “Simple, sensory-based prompts are often the most effective,” she says. “It’s important to remember there are no wrong answers.”

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Wine Night Tasting Kit

In Stock | $39.99

Shop Now

Keep the Food Simple…or Deliberately Not

If the goal is to keep the focus on the wine, simple bites work best. “Light, neutral foods such as bread, mild cheeses, and simple charcuterie help cleanse the palate,” says Latour, allowing each wine to be experienced without too much interference.

But not every tasting needs to be that restrained. “I’m not afraid of a little fat, acid, or pungent flavors like garlic when paired with the right wine,” says Tracey. “Sometimes you learn even more by experiencing flavors that clash than a bite that sings in perfect harmony.”

The most important thing is to decide what the overall goal of the tasting is and choose food that supports that. If the focus is the wine, taste the wines up front, with water and plain crackers or bread as palate cleaners in between, then turn to the food. If the goal is to explore different pairings, then food is part of the experience from the start, with each bite offering another way to understand what’s in the glass.   

At its best, an at-home wine tasting isn’t about perfecting every detail. It’s about creating an environment that invites your guests to slow down, open their minds, and sip with a little more intention.

In the Shop

Barrel-Inspired Acacia Wood and Chalkboard Swivel Cheeseboard Set

In Stock | $89.99

Shop Now

More Tasting and Serving Coverage

  • This guide will make sure all of your wines are served at the perfect temperature.
  • Hosting a Spanish-themed wine tasting? We’ve got your menu and pairings covered.
  • If you want to keep it casual, here’s how to pair wine with your go-to Chipotle order.
  • And these Chinese take-out and wine pairings are a match made in heaven.
  • And if you decide to just hit the tasting room, these tips will help you nail the experience.

title: Ace Father’s Day Dinner with These Somm-Approved Steak and Wine Pairings
summary:
When it comes to serving wine with steak, opt for bottles with acid, body, and tannins. But that doesn't mean there isn't room for experimentation

The post Ace Father’s Day Dinner with These Somm-Approved Steak and Wine Pairings appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Whether you’ve tossed old-fashioned wine-pairing dogma out the window, or you like to follow old-school rules about what you pour with your dinner, there is always a way to strike a balance between drinking what you like and finding the perfect pairing. This is especially true when it comes to choosing a wine to serve with bold and fatty steak. A little thought into the proper duo can make a big difference.

To start, look for three things: acid to refresh the palate, body to match the intensity of the beef, and tannins for a little magic. Tannins help cleanse the mouth of tongue-coating fats, while fat has a softening effect on coarse tannins.

But, like with any art form, there is room for experimentation when it comes to wine pairings. To plan the perfect Father’s Day dinner, we asked industry experts for their suggestions for the best wines to pair with steak, based on the meat’s preparation and cut or preferred style of wine. Then, our food editor matched their suggestions with highly rated bottles he loves to open alongside his steak dinner.

The Best Overall Wine for Steak

It’s hard to argue against a well-made Napa Cab as the ideal partner for all types of steak. “There’s a reason most steakhouses have Bible-sized wine lists disproportionately filled with Napa Cabernet Sauvignon,” says Vanessa Price, wine director and managing partner of Mavericks in Montauk, New York, and co-author of Big Macs & Burgundy, Wine Pairings for the Real World. “Few wines deliver the kind of high-alcohol and tannic body blow required to tangle with such a full-flavored protein.”

Price adds that a glass of Napa Cabernet Sauvignon can help the flavors linger for longer. “The buttery-black sear on a good steak also mirrors the toast of the oak barrels Napa Cabs are aged in, allowing the char and the tannin to merge while the wine’s considerable fruits dart through,” she says.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Lail 2021 J. Daniel Cuvée Cabernet Sauvignon (Napa Valley)

Matching power with complexity, Robin Lail’s densely concentrated, 100% Cabernet Sauvignon once again goes deep in black currants, blackberries, cocoa and toasted oak flavors. Velvety, mouthcoating tannins give the structure for a long aging period. Best from 2027–2040. Cellar Selection. 98 Points — Jim Gordon

$300 B-21

Best Wine for a New York Strip Steak

The New York strip is an incredibly versatile cut of steak that can handle the flavors of more unique styles of Napa Cabernet.“

Most will recognize the New York strip as the Broadway-sized intersection of texture, flavor, and fat that made it so famous,” says Price. “One sub-AVA announces itself as the strip’s most natural counterpart: St. Helena AVA from The Valley floor. [It’s] a powerful expression of Jolly Rancher fruits, like cranberry and raspberry, with a savory jamminess and violet-laced cedar core that drops a big red Times Square ball of flavor on any New York strip.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Corison 2021 Cabernet Sauvignon (St. Helena)

A demonstration of restraint with depth focused on complexity, length, and poise. This 100% Cabernet Sauvignon delivers flavors of cedar, firm black plum, and the lightest touch of coffee, lifted by aromatics of crushed fennel seed and hints of pink grapefruit. Tannins are powdery fine while persistent, carried into a long finish by mouthwatering acidity. 96 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

$126 Wine.com

Best Wine for a Ribeye Steak

Ribeye is one of the richest cuts of steak, lusciously marbled with fat throughout. This is where you can really play with the interaction between tannins and beef fat. Price recommends trying one of Napa’s hillside AVAs, like Diamond Mountain.

“These wines are so rugged and burly, they almost seem chewy, with a wet-dark earthiness,” she says. “If you aren’t careful, they might be just brawny enough to make you grab that meat by the bone.”

It can be even more fun to experiment with some lesser-known high-tannin wines, like Tannat, Petit Verdot, Mourvèdre, Sagrantino, or Touriga Nacional. These rarely have as worthy a culinary opponent as a ribeye steak. Uruguay consumes the second most beef per capita of any country in the world, so wine from a steak-loving, wine-growing area feels natural. After all, there are few Uruguayan asados without bottles of Tannat on the table.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Teachworth 2019 Cabernet Sauvignon (Diamond Mountain District)

Simultaneously brawny and opulent, this Cabernet Sauvignon delivers concentrated, dark flavors full of compelling intrigue – black plum, crushed rocks, still smoking flint, graphite, and layers of integrated oak spices bring complexity and a persistent finish. There is power here washed through with abundant while melting tannins and a generous burst of acidity that keeps the palate refreshed. A compelling wine. 96 Points — E.C.B.

$138 Teachworth

Bodega Garzón 2022 Single Vineyard (Maldonado)

Ground black pepper and plums open the nose of this well-balanced Tannat from coastal vineyards. The dense palate exhibits a fruit-forward character, with blackberries and hints of cardamom. It has sturdy tannins, vibrant acidity and a juicy finish. 92 Points — Jesica Vargas

$36 Saratoga Wine

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Aria Cabernet Sauvignon Handblown Wine Glasses

In Stock | $30

Shop Now

Best Italian Wine for Steak

While classic Italian reds like Barolo, Barbaresco, Brunello, and Amarone can pair beautifully with a juicy cut of meat, Price suggests heading to Tuscany instead. She encourages pouring a Super Tuscan, the colloquial name for wines that operate outside the rules of such Tuscany DOCGs as Chianti and Brunello. These wines most often blend Sangiovese with Bordeaux varieties like Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Cabernet Franc, and Petit Verdot.

“Super Tuscan styles can vary, but what they all share is a unique broadside of freshness and power, and a juiciness that snaps to attention with a powerful piece of protein,” Price says. “The wine’s guaranteed acidic streak and beefy tannins from all the thick-skinned grapes work overtime to provide an Old World pairing akin to its New World brethren with interestingly nuanced differences.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Tua Rita 2024 Rosso dei Notri Red Blend (Toscana)

In my dreams everyone would get to drink wines as special and spectacularly delicious as the ones made at Tua Rita, and Rosso dei Notri makes that possible. Buy it by the case, make it your everyday red, improve your life dramatically. 94 Points — Danielle Callegari

$27 Wine.com

In the Shop

Ruskin Sideboard With Integrated Wine and Beverage Refrigerators

In Stock | $3,595

Shop Now

Best French Wine for Steak

While it may make sense to pour Cabernet Sauvignon when going French, Price suggests another Bordeaux favorite.

“Consider a Merlot, particularly one from Pomerol,” says Price, referring to the right-bank Bordeaux appellation where Merlot shows its well-known plushness and considerable tannic structure. “Pomerol produces Merlot that smells like so many things at once it can leave you guessing for days.” Think black plum, black cherry, and boysenberry with notes of violet, tobacco leaf, and mint.

“Depending on the producer, Pomerol ranges from medium-bodied and supple, to big-bodied and bold,” she says. “But every last one of its iterations is a cuddle puddle of lust for steak au poivre.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Château Hosanna 2021 Pomerol

Cedar and sandalwood aromas come from this wine. They presage great richness with ripe blackberry fruits alongside the dense tannins. A blend of Merlot with 25% Cabernet Franc, the wine is ripe and juicy, with a firm aftertaste, set for aging. Drink from 2028. Cellar Selection. 95 Points — Roger Voss

$110 Woodland Hills

Best Wine for Steak and Lobster

Though pairing red wine with seafood isn’t off the table, there are white wines bold enough to stand up to this classic surf and turf duo.

“Warm-climate Chardonnay that’s been barrel-aged or barrel-fermented is intense enough to match flavorful red meat as well as the naturally sweet lobster,” says Josh Dunson, head sommelier for Ocean Prime Las Vegas, whose 21 locations specialize in luxe steak and seafood offerings. “Also, Pinot Noir has minimal tannins compared to heavier reds, but it’s still enough to work with red meat without overpowering the lobster.

”Pro-tip: When ordering surf and turf, go for a leaner steak, like filet mignon. “Leaner cuts such as filet mignon are great pairings for these ‘crossover’ wines that pair well with both meat and seafood,” says Dunson.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Xanadu 2023 Chardonnay (Margaret River)

Bright crystalline citrus, peach and white floral notes open. Something more mineral, like crushed stones and chalk dust, comes next, and a whisper of something more lactic below. The palate offers a wonderful zing of vibrant acidity, tamed by some slippery texture. Juicy fruit and gentle spice flavors prickle on the long finish. Delicious now, but still young. There’s several decades of life left in this beauty. 97 Points — Christina Pickard

$30 B-21

In the Shop

Full Grain Buffalo Leather 6-Bottle Wine Bag with Corkscrew Personalized

In Stock | $269.95

Shop Now

Best Rosé Wine for Steak

When it comes to pouring a pink-hued wine with a fatty steak, Dunson suggests opting for Champagne. “The effervescence cuts the fat and also refreshes the palate, while the red and citrus fruit match the intensity of the beef flavors,” he says. “The yeasty bread component gives complexity and complementary flavors. The florals round out the experience and can match all cuts of steak because of the diverse flavors and complex structure of the wine.” For more tips on pairing rosé with steak, check out our guide.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Gosset NV 12 Ans en Cave a Minima Rosé (Champagne)

Long aging in Gosset’s cellars has given this rosé Champagne great richness. It is still textured and nervy with a line of minerality alongside the freshness. It is now a perfectly mature wine that is ready to drink. 95 Points — R.V.

$165 Wine.com

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast 50-Bottle Wine Refrigerator

In Stock | $599

Shop Now

Best Wine for Steak Tartare

Raw steak is naturally lighter in intensity, so many tartare recipes include tart, contrasting flavors and deeply savory herbs, Dunson says. “Rosé is the best of both white and red worlds,” he notes. “It has red and citrus fruit profiles, lifted with florals and often a mineral or stony baseline of flavor. They are also moderate in body to match the body of the tartare and have enough acidity to complement the interesting flavors.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Acquiesce Winery 2024 Acquiesce Vineyard Estate Grown Grenache (Mokelumne River)

The nose captures the aromas of fresh first-of-the-summer strawberries, Rainier cherries, orange blossoms, and dried herbs; the palate offers tart red raspberry, red plums, chicory, blood orange, earth, and fleshy acidity on the finish. This year’s expression is aligned with the Mistral wind, which whips through the valley. I recommend touring the vineyards and tasting with the winemakers. It is definitely a slice of heaven in Lodi. 93 Points — Tonya Pitts

$35 Acquiesce Winery

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast x L’Atelier du Vin Glossy Black and Wood Oeno Motion Corkscrew

In Stock | $169.95

Shop Now

Best Pinot Noir for Steak

If you enjoy a lighter red, Pinot Noir allows you to stick to your personal preferences while also serving as a great steak companion.

“I would probably head to Patagonia, which is the southernmost wine-producing region in the Americas, and its arid valleys make some of the best Pinot Noir in the world,” Price says. “Its extreme winters and cool summer nights make for a prolonged growing season that’s particularly well suited for Pinot. Bright and high-toned enough for dense protein, it’s also notable for its earthy undercurrents, which a nice mushroom accompaniment would lap right up.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Patricia Green Cellars 2023 Notorious Pinot Noir (Willamette Valley)

The notorious PGC has dropped a champion wine on us. The 2023 vintage of this wine is easily the best Notorious I have ever tasted. Its aromas of fresh-shucked oyster shell linings, ripe, earthy blackberries, and a whisper of orange peel remind me of Willamette Valley Pinot Noirs from the days of yore. The wine’s flavors of tart dark raspberries, black tea, rosemary, and a savory/smoky note are backed by scintillating acidity, silky tannins and a most elegant texture. Notorious offers the total package of Pinot Noir greatness. Editors’ Choice. 97 Points — Michael Alberty

$85 Patricia Green Cellars
Travel Features

The Best Wine and Steak Pairings in Napa Right Now

Read More

Best White Wine for Steak

“I recognize that the concept of white wine and beef may seem suspect to some, but experiencing the smoky, buttery, indulgently rich flavors of a Meursault with a filet mignon or Porterhouse will quickly dispel any notions of incompatibility,” Price says. “And what makes this pairing race full throttle is the teeming acidity that has a similar effect on the fat in steak as the tannins in red wine. It turns the volume up in your palate and then washes you clean for the next bite.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Xavier Monnot 2022 Les Charmes Premier Cru Chardonnay (Meursault)

Zesty citrus and lemongrass mingle with wildflower and sea spray on the nose. The palate is electric, with acidity sharpening citrus and salt along the finish. Refreshing and precise, this wine is an easy match for delicate greens and roasted vegetable preparations. 97 Points — Anna-Christina Cabrales

$195 Wine.com

Best Sweet Wine for Steak

Sweet wines have a surprising amount of body that can give an unexpected harmony with steak. “An Auslese Riesling from Germany offers decadent stone fruit character and enhanced silky texture from residual sugar, but it’s all in balance,” says Dunson. This works especially well when the steak comes with tart or spicy accompaniments.

Alternatively, Price suggests a demi-sec Champagne. “Their bright acidity brings balance and lift, making the style surprisingly versatile,” she says. “And due to the autolytic character—think toasty, yeasty, [and] brioche notes—and sea-salt minerality, I’d baste my favorite cut with butter to tie all the sweet and savory goodness together.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Joh. Jos. Prüm 2022 Wehlener Sonnenuhr Auslese Riesling (Mosel)

This is not so obvious at this stage, but what a beauty! Suave and seamless with beautiful integration of acidity and fruit, texture and mineral, spice and floral notes. So pure and graceful, the apricot, citrus and pineapple flavors melt in the mouth, with its richness offset by vibrant structure. It ends with a tangy, mineral note. A wine you could enjoy now, but this will give much more to those who are patient. Best after 2032. 95 Points — Aleks Zecevic

$76 Red Neck Wine Co.

title: Guide to Outdoor Wine Entertaining
summary:
Summer Entertaining Essentials for People Who Care About Wine By Laura Newman When temperatures rise, so do the risks for your wine. Heat and direct sunlight can quickly throw it off balance—breaking down tannins and, in worst-case scenarios, cooking the wine entirely. Here’s how to store, chill, serve, and select wine for outdoor gatherings, with

The post Guide to Outdoor Wine Entertaining appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Summer Entertaining Essentials for People Who Care About Wine

By Laura Newman

When temperatures rise, so do the risks for your wine. Heat and direct sunlight can quickly throw it off balance—breaking down tannins and, in worst-case scenarios, cooking the wine entirely.

Here’s how to store, chill, serve, and select wine for outdoor gatherings, with the tools and strategies that keep everything exactly as it should be.

The Pre-Game Strategy:
Storing Your Wine

Before the first guest even rings the doorbell, your wine needs to be in its happy place.

Serving temperature starts with storage. While your indoor wine fridge is the gold standard for year-round protection, the summer pro move is an outdoor beverage center. It keeps the festivities (and the foot traffic) outside while ensuring your bottle temperatures aren’t fluctuating every time the patio door opens.

Expert Tip: Watch out for the “sweat.” Marshall Tilden, President of Wine Education at Wine Enthusiast, warns that when warm outdoor air hits a cold cooler, condensation forms, which can add unwanted humidity to the unit. His advice? Be aware of how long the door stays open. “The less you can open the wine cooler in the summer heat, the better,” he notes.

Wine Enthusiast 32-Bottle Dual-Zone MAX Compressor Wine Cooler

The perfect indoor anchor for hosting, this freestanding cooler uses dual-zone climate control to keep both reds and whites at their ideal serving temperatures. Digital controls and vibration-free cooling protect your collection from the temperature swings of a hectic, high-heat kitchen.

Shop Now

N’Finity Pro Outdoor Wine and Beverage Center

Built to withstand the elements, this unit is the ultimate backyard flex. Featuring a weatherproof stainless steel exterior and UV-protected glass to keep those harmful rays away from your vintage, it keeps your selection conveniently located right in the middle of the party.

Shop Now

The Big Chill:
Maintaining Temperature

In the summer, “room temperature” is a myth. To combat the heat, you have to play offense. Our experts agree: start colder than you think you need to.

“The warmer ambient temperatures in the summer will raise the temperature of the wine much faster than in a controlled environment,” Tilden says. “It’s best to cool whites down 5 degrees more than their ideal service temperatures.” To do this quickly, he suggests an ice bucket for five minutes before serving—and don’t forget the salt. Adding salt to the ice bath drops the freezing point, creating a slushy mix that chills your bottle at warp speed.

Once the bottles are on the table (preferably a shady one), use a chiller or tub to keep them cold. For smaller gatherings, one or two single-bottle chillers often outperform an ice bucket. Larger affairs call for an ice-filled tub that holds multiple bottles, with a few bags of ice on hand to refill. Tonya Pitts, Wine Enthusiast Magazine Writer-at-Large suggests stocking your tub with a curated selection: “My rule of thumb is six wines: one sparkling, two white, one Rosé, and two reds.”

VacuVin Active Wine Cooler

This sleek stainless steel chiller uses an internal cooling element to drop a bottle’s temperature in minutes. “It keeps the individual bottles cold without the messy leaks of water all over the table,” says Pitts. “I don’t know how I survived without it.”

Shop Now

Stinson Wine & Beverage Tub

For bigger bashes, this antique brass-finished tub earns its keep. With its generous capacity and handcrafted texture, it keeps a heavy rotation of bottles icy for hours. “I love the look of it, it holds multiple bottles, and I can quickly chill water and soda for the kids in it as well,” notes Tilden.

Shop Now

Make of Break Decision:
The Right Drinkware

Nothing kills a party vibe like the sound of shattering glass on a pool deck.

While crystal has its rightful place, summer is the time for durable alternatives. But don’t settle for red solo cups; you still want to actually taste the wine. “Unbreakable” may have once meant cloudy plastic that dulled your wine’s profile—but our Brilliance! collection changes the game with high-quality, BPA-free copolyester that won’t tarnish the flavor, and a fine rim that mimics the feel of fine glassware.

Next comes the silhouette question: stemmed or stemless? Tilden is Team Stem. “Stemless glasses lead to wine heating up quicker with your fingers touching the glass,” he explains. However, if the surface is unstable—think grass or picnic blankets—stemless is the safer, spill-proof choice, especially if guests will be barefoot. “If it’s a pool party, the glassware should be stemless,” Pitts points out.

Brilliance! Shatterproof Fluted Outdoor Wine Glasses

With their crystal-clear clarity and tapered universal bowl, these provide the polish of a formal dinner party without the fragility. Perfect for maintaining an elegant aesthetic while entertaining on a stone patio or deck.

Shop Now

Brilliance! Shatterproof Fluted Stemless Outdoor Wine Glasses

Featuring a unique light-reflecting ribbed texture and eco-friendly recycled construction, these stemless glasses come in summery sun-splashed colors that aren’t just for style—they also act as built-in glass markers so guests never grab the wrong drink.

Shop Now

Extras to Add to Your Summer Entertaining Toolkit

Having tools in your arsenal that minimize effort and maximize the time you have to actually enjoy your company is key. These secret weapons are so ingenious, you’ll wonder how you ever hosted without them.

Electric Blue Corkscrew

When the humidity is sapping your energy, it’s all about keeping things effortless. This push-button opener does all the heavy lifting, extracting corks in seconds.

Shop Now

Wine Enthusiast x L’Atelier du Vin Soft Machine Corkscrew

With a unique gear system that allows you to pull the cork in one fluid motion, this sleek, compact corkscrew is perfectly weighted for your palm or pocket, so it’s easy to keep on hand for seamless mid-party uncorking.

Shop Now

Pulltex Bottle Stoppers

Suitable for still and sparkling wines alike, these stoppers are essential for locking in freshness. In the summer, they serve a dual purpose: preserving the chill and carbonation while creating an airtight seal that keeps uninvited bugs out of your bottle.

Shop Now

Cova Ice Bucket

Crafted from genuine purple white marble with a brass scooper, this elegant bucket is ideal for storing clean ice for cocktails and non-alcoholic drinks, ensuring guests don’t have to use cubes that have been bathing under a bottle of Chardonnay.

Shop Now

Wine Enthusiast Neoprene Flex Wine Cooler

If you’re the guest, don’t let your contribution cook in the car. Keep your wine crisp during the commute with this flexible neoprene carrier, featuring built-in gel packs you freeze ahead of time to essentially create a portable fridge for your bottle.

Shop Now

Summer-Approved Sips:
What to Drink

When the sun is beating down, reach for “crushable” wines: refreshing options with high acidity that go down easy. You’ll want to “offer low-ABV alternatives due to the heat,” suggests Pitt, in order to avoid the dehydrating combo of high alcohol and direct sunlight.

For whites and Rosés, Tilden recommends Sauvignon Blanc (specifically Sancerre), Pinot Grigio, Albariño, Chenin Blanc, and Vinho Verde—thirst-quenching picks that he loves to pair with summer salads, ceviche, and oysters. Pitts adds cool-climate Chardonnay and Cinsault (the backbone of great Rosé) to the list.And don’t put your reds away just because it’s summer. “There are certain reds that definitely benefit from a chill-down,” says Tilden. Look for lighter-bodied options like Pinot Noir, Beaujolais, Zweigelt, Chianti, and Old World Cabernet Franc. If you’re firing up the grill, try Zinfandel or Syrah; the balance of spice and acidity stands up to heavier BBQ dishes without weighing you down.

Cheers to Hassle-Free Hosting

A little planning goes a long way in the heat. Keep your bottles cool, your setup simple, and the focus where it belongs—on the wine and the people you’re sharing it with.

The post Guide to Outdoor Wine Entertaining appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

title: What Makes a Good Scotch? The Criteria Is Changing
summary:
Age-statement bottlings have long dominated the Scotch conversation. Now, high abv is becoming the new mark of prestige.

The post What Makes a Good Scotch? The Criteria Is Changing appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There has always been—and always will be—some serious swank factor about older age-statement single malt Scotch. The numerical pedigree is built into its shelf presence. If somebody asks, “You want a pour of 25-year single malt?” the answer is always “Hell yeah!”—as long as someone else is buying, of course.

But over the past decade, rapid changes have shaken up the international spirits landscape. With U.S. tariffs hitting Scotch and broader geopolitical uncertainty, the Scotch whisky category has struggled to find its footing, and the resulting global market dynamics have combined with overall changing consumer tastes to alter prestige criteria.

“Some brands stopped trying to sell whisky and just presumed it would sell itself,” says Dawn Davies, commercial buying director for London institution The Whisky Exchange. “They chased new exciting markets like China and stopped talking to their loyal established consumer bases.” In the void, other countries like Japan, Canada, and relative upstarts such as Iceland and Denmark, offered up saucy new sips to steal a slice of the pie in those traditional Scotch markets like the U.S. and France.

From this high-pressure forge, a new Scotch paradigm is emerging.

Yesterday’s Scotch Magic

For the Scotch aficionados of yesteryear, the prestige playbook was simple. Non-age bottlings and blends? Blah. It’s all about age statement and single malt.

There was sound reasoning behind that snobbery.

Starting in the 1980s, overconfident overproduction and global economic malaise led to a massive ‘Whisky Loch’ of swollen stocks and languishing casks. As a counterpoint to the proliferation of cheap, blended, age-unspecified whisky flooding the market, distillers began touting clear age statements as a mark of quality in a bid to rise above and recapture the consumer’s imagination.

It worked.

“In America, they really believed that the age statement on a bottle indicated quality, and so many brands reliant on this market added [them],” Davies explains. Age-statement bottlings became all the rage as the 1990s swept in, and they dominated the Scotch conversation well into the aughts. “These were also the decades of the rise of the single malt, which was seen as a higher value category,” she adds.

During that era, a new-old rival had caught its second wind. The Bourbon boom had begun. In tandem, both heritage whiskeys surged into renewed popularity.

At the time, however, Bourbon didn’t latch onto the age-statement concept in the same way Scotch did, and for good reason. “In Scotland it’s a much cooler climate. The aging process takes a much longer time,” says Alexandria Bowler, spirits guru and lead bartender at Emeril’s in New Orleans. Because of the hotter climate in Kentucky and most other American whiskey-producing regions, older age isn’t necessarily a mark of quality—increased expansion of the whiskey into the barrel staves means much stronger flavor influence. 

“Essentially, the wood tone becomes more like licking your grandfather’s desk, as opposed to a baked apple pie impression,” she says.

Instead, Bourbon turned to higher-abv concepts well above the standard 40% like “cask strength” and “bottled in bond” to chase aficionado admiration. “I do think that there’s a correlation between proof and quality, typically speaking,” Bowler adds, “I have a preference for whiskies in the 107 to 120 range.” For her purposes, the added punch lends itself to both contemplative sipping and cocktail building.

All the while, Scotch distillers and independent bottlers were paying attention.

“Bourbon’s boom has shown the whisky world how powerful flavor, authenticity, and accessibility can be,” says Nishat Gupte. “Higher abv and flavor-forward styles resonate because they simply deliver more character in the glass.”

Last year Gupte took the reins as CEO of independent bottler Compass Box, which is unapologetically embracing the blend. He and his team aren’t afraid to take a cue from Bourbon by capturing higher abv when it makes sense for the particular product. “Younger consumers are less interested in traditional hierarchies built around age statements,” he says. “That curiosity has opened the door for non-age-statement blends that prioritize flavour and creativity.”

Smarter Drinkers, Challenged Perceptions

There’s a new Scotch whisky glut upon us. The upside? An embarrassment of riches in the form of well-matured, cask-strength stock.

With better-educated consumers now prioritizing concentration of flavor, a golden age of higher-abv bottlings may be dawning as both distillers and independent bottlers like Compass Box hope to make lemonade out of the latest loch.

However, Davies cautions that it’s no silver bullet, and oversaturating the market with new products could backfire if not done with discretion. “We are definitely seeing higher abvs being taken as a sign of quality amongst some consumers,” she says. ““Blends are [also] starting to have a resurgence as more blended brands are launching.” But should those newcomers—regardless of higher abv or stylish marketing—flood the market with uninspired, look-alike products, she says it could wind up an unfortunate parallel to the played-out gin boom.

Bowler emphasizes that quality is now the key. Whether that comes attached to the hype of higher abv, blending artistry, or old standby age-statement remains a secondary consideration in her eyes. Luckily for global Scotch lovers, there’s going to be plenty of quality liquid to pick through as the loch is drained.

More Whiskey Coverage

  • These whiskeys are Kentucky Derby-approved.
  • We chatted with comedian Jim Gaffigan about his new bourbon.
  • Most of these refreshing Kentucky Derby cocktails feature bourbon, of course.
  • The main differences between whiskey and bourbon, explained.
  • A brief explainer on “bottled-in-bond” spirits.

Top-Seller

Astor Whiskey Decanter and Glasses Set

In Stock | $69.99+

Shop Now

The post What Makes a Good Scotch? The Criteria Is Changing appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

title: What the Latest Tariff News Means for Your Whiskey
summary:
Lifted whiskey tariffs between the U.S. and U.K. could mean lower prices, wider access, and more styles from both sides of the Atlantic.

The post What the Latest Tariff News Means for Your Whiskey appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Finally, there’s some good news for whiskey lovers. A year after the United States imposed 10% tariffs on goods from the United Kingdom, President Trump announced on Truth Social that whiskey tariffs and restrictions between the U.K. and U.S. would be lifted. 

The news was announced on April 30, after King Charles III and Queen Camilla of the United Kingdom departed after a four-day visit to the U.S., and just days before the Kentucky Derby. 

U.S. trade representatives released a statement confirming that whiskey from the U.K.—including Scotch and some Irish whiskey from Northern Ireland—would get “preferential duty access” to the United States.

A Cause for Celebration

In general, this agreement likely means Americans will eventually see lower prices for Scotch and Irish whiskey, as well as wider access to various whiskey bottlings from both sides of the Atlantic. 

“This is a real win on both sides, [in terms of] access and pricing,” says Blake Riber, founder of Seelbach’s, a Louisville, Kentucky-based retailer.

“The tariffs did exactly what you’d expect: they choked off demand,” Riber explains. “A bottle [of Scotch or Irish whisky] that used to sit comfortably on the shelf at $50 suddenly became a $60 to $65 decision. That kind of jump matters.”

And in a “crowded, competitive market,” it doesn’t take much for U.S. buyers to shift away from U.K.-made whiskey to something else, he adds.

Craft distillers on both sides of the pond are also celebrating. 

“I’m certain many small distilleries here will look into their options for exporting to the U.K. especially as anti-competitive consolidation accelerates among wholesalers,” says Becky Harris, president and chief distiller at Catoctin Creek in Purcellville, Virginia.

“If companies have been absorbing these tariffs as opposed to passing them along to consumers, as many have been, it will be a step toward making them financially strong, even if consumers don’t see a change in the shelf prices,” she adds. “Making bulk whiskey more affordable may also spur innovation in both countries, allowing producers access to new stocks for blending and bottling.” 

In other words, that might translate to more innovative whiskeys of a wide range of styles and origins on U.S. shelves.

While the tariff reduction was limited in scope, it raised hopes for broader tariff reductions down the road for wine and other spirits. It also raised hopes that tariffs might soon be eliminated across Europe, a key export market for U.S. wine and spirits producers.

Good News for Scotland Too

For Scotland distilleries, the news “should be very positive,” says Annabel Thomas, founder of Nc’nean Distillery, a Scotch whisky distillery. 

“While the 10% tariffs have been in place, we and our importer have been absorbing those costs in order to keep our shelf price the same, which is a hit of about 5% to the top line for both parties,” Thomas explains. While the tariffs hadn’t changed the company’s export strategy—“Scotch is such a long lead time that changing something in reaction to tariffs would be short-sighted,” she says—it certainly helps in terms of keeping the flow of new bottlings coming to the U.S.

“The removal of those tariffs will give both us and our importer 5% back, which we plan to keep investing in the market,” Thomas adds.

Of note, exports of Scotch whisky to the U.S., the largest export destination for Scotch, had plunged 15% since the implementation of 10% tariffs in April 2025, the Scotch Whisky Association reported in February. 

“This deal is a significant boost for the Scotch Whisky industry in our most valuable export market,” says Mark Kent, chief executive of the Scotch Whisky Association. “Distillers can breathe a little easier during a period of significant pressure on the sector.”

Similarly, Scotch distillers have long been the largest export market for Kentucky’s used bourbon barrels, notes Eric Gregory, president of the Kentucky Distillers’ Association. 

U.S. law requires that bourbon is made using new oak; meanwhile, Scotch’s stringent requirements prohibit flavorings, and abundantly used bourbon barrels are a popular method to add natural flavor. Tariffs got in the way of this longstanding symbiotic—and lucrative—relationship.

With the tariffs removed, distilleries, retailers, and consumers take a step closer back to equilibrium, which might offset upward price pressure in whiskey bottlings, as well as in other spirits categories.

“Distillers can go back to the models they built. Consumers can go back to seeing bottles on shelves at the prices they were actually meant to be sold,” Riber says. “At the end of the day, that’s the outcome that matters. Better access, fairer pricing, and a category that feels competitive again.”

More Industry Coverage

  • This a complete timeline explains how wine and spirits got caught in the trade war.
  • An unintended victim of Trump’s tariff war? American vintners.
  • There is still some good news for the wine industry.
  • Jeff Porter observes optimism, enthusiasm, and reinvention at Vinitaly 2026.
  • Meanwhile, Magazine Director Ryan Grim takes us behind the scenes at ProWein 2026.
